title: Alibaba's Qwen and ByteDance's Doubao begin disabling AI companion features ahead of new Beijing rules
subtitle: Alibaba took humanlike Qwen interactive agents offline on July 10, with the wider agent-creation feature shutting July 15 and ByteDance's Doubao personas following the same day.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Alibaba disabled its 'humanlike interactive agents' and user-created agent functions inside its Qwen products on July 10, with the broader agent-creation feature scheduled to shut down on July 15. ByteDance's Doubao will retire its user-customizable AI persona feature on the same day, and Tencent-linked platforms are pulling back on similar features. The synchronized shutdowns respond to new rules from Beijing that specifically target AI systems built to simulate human personalities, relationships, and emotional interaction.

Chinese regulators have framed the concern as consumer harm — persuasive or emotionally responsive chatbots creating unhealthy dependency, impersonating real people, or generating content that violates content rules. The rules build on China's existing generative AI framework, which since 2023 has required licensed models, real-name registration, and content review for public-facing services. What is new is the explicit carve-out of 'humanlike agents' as a category to be tightly restricted, mirroring the direction of the EU's AI Act around emotion recognition and manipulative design.

The economics are meaningful. Companion and roleplay use cases have been among the most consistent revenue drivers for consumer AI apps in Chinese and Japanese markets — Character.ai and Talkie-style products, not enterprise agents, are what actually keeps users paying subscriptions. By restricting the format at the app-store and model-provider level, Beijing is choosing to sacrifice a large consumer-AI revenue category in exchange for tighter control over the emotional attack surface of large models. That is a different bet than the US or EU has made so far.

Takeaway for learners: 'AI regulation' is not one thing. The US is focused on frontier-model national security, the EU on rights and transparency, and China on social control of the human-facing layer. If you are building an AI product with any international ambition, the smart move is to study which of these regimes bites hardest on your specific use case — because a companion app that ships in California may be illegal in Shanghai and unmarketable in Berlin.

sources.title: ByteDance and Alibaba to disable humanlike AI custom agents as new rules loom — South China Morning Post
ByteDance and Alibaba are disabling AI companion features ahead of new China rules — Quartz
ByteDance, Tencent, and Alibaba Are Shutting Down AI Chat Companions — Pandaily
